speaker: interviewer
text: Or not? You probably didn't want to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ohrdruf. Ohrdruf was like, you came with a group to Ohrdruf. When they transferred the group, you were among the group. You were a member of a group. So that particular group was transferred to another camp called [? Garfinkel. ?] And then [? Garfinkel ?] was not too far away, maybe 50 kilometers. And the same thing happened. We were newly arrived into [? Garfinkel ?] and there was no specific work to do. And we were cleaning the highways, taking the snow from one area to another area, just wasting time. The war is almost coming to an end. It was already March of 1945. Then they liquidated the camp of [? Garfinkel ?] and took us back to Buchenwald. By that time, the group was emaciated. There was maybe about 200 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came back to Buchenwald, into the small camps. And the conditions were very bad. It was cold. It was freezing. And we had the kind of lodging, like this situation that the pictures I showed you, like in Birkenau. We used to get in with the feet first and the heads like a row of sardines, laying one next to the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the morning, you get up-- your partner, your next door neighbor was dead. So I wake up in the morning and two guys beside me were dead. We had to drag them out and pile them up on the place there. And they took them away. And we don't know what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was day in and day out.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just died slow deaths of starvation?

speaker: interviewee
text: They died-- people died so fast, day in and and day out. There was very little food. We had to stay in an Appell. Appell means outside. They used to count how many people are alive, how many people are dead. And until this camp commander came to count us, many of us froze to death. I was lucky. I don't know how I survived. Anyways, and we stayed there until-- I think it was in the very beginning of April. April, 1945, which was about I think the 5th or the 6th of April, they rounded up all the people on the small-- the newly-arrived people from the subcamp of Auschwitz and took them to a-- to the train. And they put us on a train going-- we didn't know where we're going. In fact, after the liberation I found out that Buchenwald as a camp was liberated on April 11th by the American Army. But in the meantime, we were on a train going to nowhere. And on that way, going to nowhere-- we did not know where we were going-- an American plane were shooting at our train and disabled the locomotive. And some German soldiers that used to guard us got killed, and also many prisoners. So they made us leave the train. And I don't remember the name of the place. But they made us leave the train. There were many people that got killed by the Americans on that train, which was unfortunate. And we started marching. And we were marching, going nowhere. But on the way marching we found out that we were in Bavaria. And this-- we stopped over at one night in a camp. I don't remember the name of the camp. It was only for about two days. And then we got out. We were marching. And on the way we were marching, the camp-- the peo-- the commanding officer on the march asking for volunteers. Because many of the people that were marching fell dead on the road. And we had to bury them. So he was asking for volunteers to dig graves to bury the people that fell dead. So I was a volunteer to go to work. And the reason I was volunteering is I thought that maybe I'm going to meet a civilian. But every time I meet a civilian, I somehow talk him into bringing me some food. But as luck had it, I didn't have  a chance to meet any civilians right away. But that was April the 21st, 1945. I was digging graves in Bavaria on the side of a road. And there were-- I had four other people, Hungarian Jews that didn't speak Yiddish. And I had a hard time conversing with them, but somehow we made it happen. And we were digging graves together. We used to put in six to eight people in a grave. And on the night of the 22nd, following night, about 12 o'clock at night, it was raining so strongly that I asked the guard who was guarding us that we cannot work any longer. And I told him, we have to go into a farm to dry out and get something to eat. Otherwise, we're going to have to be put into these graves ourselves. And he-- he agreed. But he told me, but you have to knock on the door and you have to answer the question. I says, I will. And he saw that I have-- you know, I have a spade in my hand, a shovel. And it was raining, so he has his [? carabine ?] turned around. And he was covered up with a raincoat. So he couldn't move. If he said no, I don't know what I would have done, because I was very serious. And he saw in my eyes that it's-- I'm very, very serious at the moment. It was a life and death situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knocked at the door in a farmhouse. And a German woman-- the farmer's wife or whatever she was-- she opened the door. And I ask her that I-- I really didn't ask her. I really almost demanded that we should-- she should let us in to dry out. She says, absolutely, no question about it. She welcomed us. And she brought hay and put it on a floor in the kitchen and in their living room. She brought out blankets, put the blanket on, and gave us blankets to cover each-- every one of us, including the soldier that-- the guard that guarded us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she made us a very thin potato soup, a hot potato soup. And she dried our clothes. She washed it and dried it. And she made us eat that soup. And we fell asleep like little babies. [LAUGHS] We didn't care what happened. And the following morning, about 6 o'clock in the morning, we got up and she gave us our clothes back, dried. And we were of the opinion that we're going to have to go and dig graves again. So when we got up, she made us another portion of soup-- thin soup-- and she also gave us a piece of bread. She says, eat that. It's not much, but I don't want you to have much. She was like-- she was-- seems to be knowledgeable, what she was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went out from that farmhouse to the road. And we were-- we thought that we we're going to dig graves. But the soldier-- the guard, says, we're not going to dig graves anymore. Let the community deal-- the local people-- let them do it. We were going to catch up to the group And that was early morning on April the 23rd, 1945. And the name of the community that we reached-- the name of it is called Neunberg vorm Wald, in Bavaria. And the moment we-- that group of mine-- we five people-- we reached the rest of the group, we turned around and there were no Germans, no guards. Where did they all go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were white flags in the-- hanging out from the little homes, from the farm houses. And we didn't know what's going to happen. We thought that the German Army are going to come back and the American Army or whoever army is going to come after them. So we were a little afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a few men that I was in the coal mines with. We went into a little forest-- each in Germany, right in front of every little community, they have like a small forest. So we went into that forest and we lie down and we waited. We didn't know what's going to happen. And we heard some shooting into that little forest, but we didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the people that were there were an ex-soldier in the Polish Army. So they said, lay down. Don't say anything. Then after a while, I had a towel for a shawl. So I took my shawl, my towel, put it on a twig, and we went outside going like, we give up. We surrender. But instead of German soldiers, there were Americans, and the American on tanks-- big American tanks. And there were Jewish soldiers, speaking Yiddish, from Brooklyn. And he told us, don't be afraid anymore. You don't need it. You are free. And they told us they liberated other camps, so they know what's going on. And they warned us not to eat, because nurses are coming, and doctors are coming, and they will take care of us. And please don't eat. But at the same time, they gave us chocolate and cans of food, C-rations. He said, you're going to have this for later. But I didn't eat. But there were some people in the group that ate and died because they ate. So unfortunately, they lived to be liberated by the American Army and they didn't have the patience to wait just another hour or so. And that was the day I was liberated-- April the 23rd, 1945 in Neunberg vorm Wald in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have the patience because you thought of it yourself or because the woman had told you that earlier-- don't eat too much?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, I was lucky because I happened to be in that farm. And the woman gave me some food, and I wasn't that deprived anymore. I felt like I had something in my stomach. And I listened to the American soldier. And I realized that he had a point there. Because the woman told me we should eat very small portions in the beginning to get our stomachs used to food. And I took her advice. Personally, I was-- you know, I was rationalizing. She's absolutely right. She's correct. So that was the day of my liberation. And I usually celebrate that day as a new birthday. So actually, I was born April the 23rd, 1945. I'm a young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Now, you went back to Poland afterwards to look for your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I organized a group of boys in that little place, Neunberg vorm Wald. There were of one Jewish boy and myself-- about twenty boys. And we decided we're going to go back to Warsaw because I hoped that my brother is going to be alive. And we got the-- in July-- that was like, April, three months later. We were already recuperated. We felt good. We were young. And in July, they gave us a truck, an American truck with an American driver, a soldier. And he was supposed to take us to the Russian-- the Russian frontier. Immediately Germany was divided into American zone and Russian zone. And in order to go to Poland, you had to go from an American zone to the Russian zone, and then from the Russian zone to Poland. So instead of going to the Russian zone, they took us to a camp. The camp was situated in the city called Fulda. And also in Germany-- no, about 100 kilometers from Frankfurt am Main-- and this camp was a Displaced Person camp run by the Polish government in exile. And their headquarter was in London. And we got there. And they treated us very nicely. They gave us food and everything. And the Polish officers came in from London. And they warned us not to go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because our aim was to go back to Poland. That's what we told the American authorities. And they decided to try to indoctrinate us not to go back to Poland, because the Russian army is there, and they mistreat the population, and they kill, and they maim, and they rape. They take everything away. It's not safe to go. Why don't we wait until it's going to be safe? Every-- all the Polacks are going to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the next day, we broke the fence, cut the fence out-- because there was a guard who didn't let us out. So we cut the fence. It wasn't an electric fence. It was a regular fence. And we just got out, one by one. And then we came to a bridge. And the bridge-- there was an American soldier guarding all the bridges because they didn't want the-- they didn't know what's going to happen. You know, but the bridge was formed to make sure that the American trucks go in safely-- go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he asked us, hey, where are you going? So I told him, we just got liberated by the American Army. Were going home. He says, go! So we somehow got transportation from place to place. Farmers took us on horse rides with hay. And then eventually we got to, on the Russian side, a city called Kemnitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kemnitz, we met the Russian officers, Jewish officers. And I could talk to them both. Russia and-- well, a little bit Russian, but mostly Jewish. And they gave us accommodations. They went in, into a hotel and they kicked everybody out. [LAUGHS] Everybody got a room. And they brought us food and everything. Then they took us the next-- the next day to the railroad, put us on a train. We went to the Polish frontier. And [INAUDIBLE]-- and we had to go-- from that passenger train we had to get off on the Polish border. And on the Polish border was also Russians. Somehow they divided the territory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went into the flatbed. And on the flatbed, we-- everybody had some belongings, some kind of clothes, whatever-- the Americans gave us. And they went into the German homes. And they said, take whatever you want, because we didn't have anything. So we take suitcases. We put it in some stuff-- you know, pants, shoes, whatever we found that fit-- took this. So and I had a-- I had a bicycle that I also took from a German. Well, they-- the Americans said, take whatever you want. So I took that bicycle all the way to Poland. And I fell asleep on that flatbed. And I get up in the morning and the bicycle was-- now, the bicycle was taken away by a Russian soldier. And he said, it's military goods. It's a form of transportation that you cannot have. It was just an excuse to take away the bike. And then half of my belongings was missing. So I went into the local commander, who was also a Jewish guy-- everybody was Jewish. All the officers-- every officer was Jewish. So he says, well, I can give you 10 soldiers to go look for it. What good is it going to do? While you look for the other suitcases, somebody's going to steal this one. He says, once they took it, you can't get it back. He said, don't waste time. Go back to Poland. So I said for myself, maybe he's right. See, when the Russian Army came into Poland, they didn't come just as soldiers by themselves. They had their wives, their children, their girlfriends-- everybody. Anyways, what happened is you didn't know who they were. So they were Russians. The soldier had his wife, his mother, his uncle, his aunt. Everybody was in the army with them as civilians. They were occupation forces. So they were stealing. They were robbing. They did everything. Finally, I went to a big city like Lodz. I went to Lodz. And I had some friends of mine in Lodz that I was in camp with. And I stayed at their place. It was funny. Before I found them, I went to the Jewish community. And I went in Lodz to the Jewish community. And it was night time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 5 o'clock, 6 o'clock at night. And it was-- got a little bit dark. And I was sitting at the door of the Jewish community, figuring that I'm going to sit there until the morning, until they open the offices. Came a lady, a Polish lady, and she looked at me. And she says to me that, I know you are Jewish and Polish. And she says, I was married to a Jewish man that the Nazis took away and never came home. And I know-- I realize that you are waiting for the offices to be open, but they are not going to be open until tomorrow morning. So if you want to come to my house, I have a room that I am forced to give to a Polish officer. But the officer went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the room is empty and you can stay overnight. But I know that you don't trust me. So I have a couple visiting somebody-- a Jewish couple. Wait until they come down and they will vouch for me. And that's exactly the way it was. So I was-- well I had-- she was waiting for that couple anyway. So the couple come down. And they guarantee me that no problem, that whatever she told me, it's true. So I went with her to her apartment. And she had a room ready for me. And I took a shower and washed myself, cleaned myself, and I got almost ready to go to bed, the Polish officer come back-- not only by herself, but he brought maybe four other guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they brought a case of vodka and food and we celebrated my liberation. They celebrated my liberation all night. And I was drunk. I didn't know what happened. [LAUGHS] Anyway, the following morning, I took off, thanked them very much. It was a very unusual evening-- night, rather. And I went to the center-- the Jewish center in Warsaw-- in Lodz. And I found people that I was in camp with. And they opened a grocery store right away. But anyways, I wasn't staying. That's not the reason I came back to Poland. I went to Warsaw. And I went to Czestochowa, to Katowice, to a lot of different cities in Poland, looking for my brother. And I couldn't find him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I registered my name everywhere and in case he registered his name, so he knows I'm alive. And then I sold everything I had on my back, except one shirt and whatever I had on. And they had a market that you could buy German marks for Polish zlotys. You see, the German-- Germany occupied Poland, right? And there was a lot of German marks. And the Polacks didn't have any use for German marks. So you could buy, let's see, 10,000 marks for 1 zloty. This was nothing. And they gave you a lot of money for your socks, and for your pants, and whatever I didn't need, I sold. And I put all the money around my body. And I went to the Czechoslovakian border to go back to Germany. So I didn't have any papers, but I had a paper that I was liberated in Bavaria. So the Polish guard on the border says, where is your papers? Oh, I took out a paper. I gave him my paper. He says, I can't read that. So I figure even if he brings his superior who could read, he can tell that this is not exactly a passport to go to Czechoslovakia. So I left him holding the paper, I turned around, and I jumped up the train that was on the Czechoslovakian side-- Slovakia then, on the Polish frontier. And I locked myself in the toilet. And I was sitting there for about an hour until the train left. And in a little while I went-- I was in Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] That was the story. From Prague, I went to Germany. There was also an interesting sequel to that, that in Prague the Jewish boys told me what to do. He said, now that you're in Prague, go to the Polish consul and tell them that you're on your way to Warsaw. They'll give you money. They'll give you a lodging in a hotel. And they'll give you a permit to stay in Prague for 10 days. And also you'll be able to go to a doctor for an examination at no charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the Polish consul and I told them, I just came from Germany. I'm on my way to Poland. He says, oh, wonderful. And he did exactly what they told me he's going to do. And he gave me some money. Because I didn't have the Czechoslovakian kroner they use-- and also food stamps. At that time, they still had food stamps. Well, it was 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had all these privileges. And I went to the doctor. And the doctor pronounced me fit-- nothing wrong. It was a very lucky occasion for me to know. And then I went to the Jewish organization that organized transportation to Germany. And their objective was to get all the Jewish boys, and girls, and families that came through Czechoslovakia from Poland and Russia to Germany. So from Germany, they should go register-- either go to Israel-- Palestine at that time-- or America or wherever. At least they'll be out of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Joint and the highest whoever was there worked with all the Jewish survivors to get out of Poland as fast as they can. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was a bad place to be after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany wasn't? Germany-- it was worse than Germany after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's worse. It was more dangerous to be in Poland than in Germany. In Germany, they organized displaced person camps. In Poland, they tried to kill you. Jewish people in Poland were in danger. So many Jewish people in Poland joined the police and various communist organizations in order to be safe-- very dangerous life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you-- tell us about how you moved between Paris and Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was a different time. Right now I am telling you that the way we completed the exit out of Poland, back to Germany, through Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop here for a minute.
